Plaintiff'S Motion Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff'S Request For Production Of Documents
Off Calendar Nov-28-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF WENCESLAO VASQUEZ Plaintiff'S Motion Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff'S Request For Production Of Documents.

Off-calendar.

The Court's Interim Discovery Order dated October 10, 2018 required the parties to meet and confer as to the documents which remained at-issue. Not having heard from the parties to date, the Court assumes that all objections and related disputes have been resolved. Accordingly, absent a showing to the contrary, the motion to compel production of documents will be placed off calendar.

Judge Pro Tem: James Fleming, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not reported. =(302/JPT)

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-03-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Atty. Jonathan M. Shugart of Erskine Law Group, Tel. No. 1-949-777-6032 appearing for
the defendant by telephone appearance;
No other appearances on the matter.

RULING - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Defendant General Motors LLC's motion to strike the punitive damages allegations is granted with leave to amend. The only claim that supports punitive damages is the fraud claim. The civil penalties under the Song-Beverly Act are a distinct remedy. (Civil Code 1794(c); Kwan v. Mercedes-Benz of N. America (1994) 23 Cal.App.4th 174, 184.) Prevailing party to prepare a form of order. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR# 8600 =302/hk

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-03-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Atty. Jonathan M. Shugart of Erskine Law Group, Tel. No. 1-949-777-6032 appearing for
the defendant by telephone appearance;
No other appearances on the matter.

RULING - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT GENERAL MOTORS LLC DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT - Defendant General Motors LLC's demurrer to the seventh cause of action for fraud by omission in the complaint filed by plaintiff Wenceslao Vazquez is sustained with twenty days leave to amend. Mr. Vasquez has leave to allege his discovery of the alleged fraud with more particularity. Paragraphs 8-9 and 31 indicate that the defects manifested within a year of the purchase. Mr. Vasquez was arguably on inquiry notice at that time and the claim appears time barred per CCP section 338. "In order to rely on the discovery rule for delayed accrual of a cause of action, '[a] plaintiff whose complaint shows on its face that his claim would be barred without the benefit of the discovery rule must specifically plead facts to show (1) the time and manner of discovery and (2) the inability to have made earlier discovery despite reasonable diligence.' [Citation] In assessing the sufficiency of the allegations of delayed discovery, the court places the burden on the plaintiff to 'show diligence'; 'conclusory allegations will not withstand demurrer.'" (Fox v. Ethicon Endo-Surgery, Inc. (2005) 35 Cal.4th 797, 808.) As explained in Cansino v. Bank of America (2014) 224 Cal.App.4th 1462, 1472: "Because the discovery rule operates as an exception to the statute of limitations, if an action is brought more than three years after commission of the fraud, plaintiff has the burden of pleading and proving that he did not make the discovery until within three years prior to the filing of his complaint. To excuse failure to discover the fraud within three years after its commission, a plaintiff also must plead facts showing that he was not negligent in failing to make the discovery sooner and that he had no actual or presumptive knowledge of facts sufficient to put him on inquiry. To that end, a plaintiff must allege facts showing "the time and surrounding circumstances of the discovery and what the discovery was. Conclusory allegations will not withstand a demurrer. The discovery related facts should be pleaded in detail to allow the court to determine whether the fraud should have been discovered sooner." (Citations and internal quotation marks omitted). GM's substantive attack on the fraud by omission claim fails. Paragraphs 50-51 show a duty to disclose based on GM's exclusive knowledge and active concealment. (Heliotis v. Schuman (1986) 181 Cal.App.3d 646, 651.) Paragraph 49 alleges that the defective cooling system posed a safety hazard. The economic loss rule argument does not apply if Mr. Vazquez is able to plead a viable non-time barred fraud claim. Prevailing party to prepare a form of order.
Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR# 8600 =302/hk
